title: Eloisa To Abelard
content:
In these deep solitudes and awful cells,
Where heavï¿½nly-pensive contemplation dwells,
And ever-musing melancholy reigns;
What means this tumult in a vestalï¿½s veins?
Why rove my thoughts beyond this last retreat?
Why feels my heart its long-forgotten heat?
Yet, yet I love!ï¿½From Abelard it came,
And Eloisa yet must kiss the name.

Dear fatal name! rest ever unrevealï¿½d,
Nor pass these lips in holy silence sealï¿½d.
Hide it, my heart, within that close disguise,
Where mixï¿½d with Godï¿½s, his lovï¿½d idea lies:
O write it not, my handï¿½the name appears
Already writtenï¿½wash it out, my tears!
In vain lost Eloisa weeps and prays,
Her heart still dictates, and her hand obeys.

Relentless walls! whose darksome round contains
Repentant sighs, and voluntary pains:
Ye rugged rocks! which holy knees have worn;
Ye grots and caverns shaggï¿½d with horrid thorn!
Shrines! where their vigils pale-eyï¿½d virgins keep,
And pitying saints, whose statues learn to weep!
Though cold like you, unmovï¿½d, and silent grown,
I have not yet forgot myself to stone.
All is not Heavï¿½nï¿½s while Abelard has part,
Still rebel nature holds out half my heart;
Nor prayï¿½rs nor fasts its stubborn pulse restrain,
Nor tears, for ages, taught to flow in vain.

Soon as thy letters trembling I unclose,
That well-known name awakens all my woes.
Oh name for ever sad! for ever dear!
Still breathï¿½d in sighs, still usherï¿½d with a tear.
I tremble too, whereï¿½er my own I find,
Some dire misfortune follows close behind.
Line after line my gushing eyes oï¿½erflow,
Led through a sad variety of woe:
Now warm in love, now withï¿½ring in thy bloom,
Lost in a conventï¿½s solitary gloom!
There stern religion quenchï¿½d thï¿½ unwilling flame,
There died the best of passions, love and fame.

Yet write, oh write me all, that I may join
Griefs to thy griefs, and echo sighs to thine.
Nor foes nor fortune take this powï¿½r away;
And is my Abelard less kind than they?
Tears still are mine, and those I need not spare,
Love but demands what else were shed in prayï¿½r;
No happier task these faded eyes pursue;
To read and weep is all they now can do.

Then share thy pain, allow that sad relief;
Ah, more than share it! give me all thy grief.
Heavï¿½n first taught letters for some wretchï¿½s aid,
Some banishï¿½d lover, or some captive maid;
They live, they speak, they breathe what love inspires,
Warm from the soul, and faithful to its fires,
The virginï¿½s wish without her fears impart,
Excuse the blush, and pour out all the heart,
Speed the soft intercourse from soul to soul,
And waft a sigh from Indus to the Pole.

Thou knowï¿½st how guiltless first I met thy flame,
When Love approachï¿½d me under Friendshipï¿½s name;
My fancy formï¿½d thee of angelic kind,
Some emanation of thï¿½ all-beauteous Mind.
Those smiling eyes, attempï¿½ring evï¿½ry day,
Shone sweetly lambent with celestial day.
Guiltless I gazï¿½d; heavï¿½n listenï¿½d while you sung;
And truths divine came mended from that tongue.
From lips like those what precept failï¿½d to move?
Too soon they taught me ï¿½twas no sin to love.
Back through the paths of pleasing sense I ran,
Nor wishï¿½d an Angel whom I lovï¿½d a Man.
Dim and remote the joys of saints I see;
Nor envy them, that heavï¿½n I lose for thee.

How oft, when pressï¿½d to marriage, have I said,
Curse on all laws but those which love has made!
Love, free as air, at sight of human ties,
Spreads his light wings, and in a moment flies,
Let wealth, let honour, wait the wedded dame,
August her deed, and sacred be her fame;
Before true passion all those views remove,
Fame, wealth, and honour! what are you to Love?
The jealous God, when we profane his fires,
Those restless passions in revenge inspires;
And bids them make mistaken mortals groan,
Who seek in love for aught but love alone.
Should at my feet the worldï¿½s great master fall,
Himself, his throne, his world, Iï¿½d scorn ï¿½em all:
Not Caesarï¿½s empress would I deign to prove;
No, make me mistress to the man I love;
If there be yet another name more free,
More fond than mistress, make me that to thee!
Oh happy state! when souls each other draw,
When love is liberty, and nature, law:
All then is full, possessing, and possessï¿½d,
No craving void left aching in the breast:
Evï¿½n thought meets thought, ere from the lips it part,
And each warm wish springs mutual from the heart.
This sure is bliss (if bliss on earth there be)
And once the lot of Abelard and me.

Alas, how changï¿½d! what sudden horrors rise!
A naked lover bound and bleeding lies!
Where, where was Eloise? her voice, her hand,
Her poniard, had opposï¿½d the dire command.
Barbarian, stay! that bloody stroke restrain;
The crime was common, common be the pain.
I can no more; by shame, by rage suppressï¿½d,
Let tears, and burning blushes speak the rest.

Canst thou forget that sad, that solemn day,
When victims at yon altarï¿½s foot we lay?
Canst thou forget what tears that moment fell,
When, warm in youth, I bade the world farewell?
As with cold lips I kissï¿½d the sacred veil,
The shrines all tremblï¿½d, and the lamps grew pale:
Heavï¿½n scarce believï¿½d the conquest it surveyï¿½d,
And saints with wonder heard the vows I made.
Yet then, to those dread altars as I drew,
Not on the Cross my eyes were fixï¿½d, but you:
Not grace, or zeal, love only was my call,
And if I lose thy love, I lose my all.
Come! with thy looks, thy words, relieve my woe;
Those still at least are left thee to bestow.
Still on that breast enamourï¿½d let me lie,
Still drink delicious poison from thy eye,
Pant on thy lip, and to thy heart be pressï¿½d;
Give all thou canstï¿½and let me dream the rest.
Ah no! instruct me other joys to prize,
With other beauties charm my partial eyes,
Full in my view set all the bright abode,
And make my soul quit Abelard for God.

Ah, think at least thy flock deserves thy care,
Plants of thy hand, and children of thy prayï¿½r.
From the false world in early youth they fled,
By thee to mountains, wilds, and deserts led.
You raisï¿½d these hallowï¿½d walls; the desert smilï¿½d,
And Paradise was openï¿½d in the wild.
No weeping orphan saw his fatherï¿½s stores
Our shrines irradiate, or emblaze the floors;
No silver saints, by dying misers givï¿½n,
Here bribï¿½d the rage of ill-requited heavï¿½n:
But such plain roofs as piety could raise,
And only vocal with the Makerï¿½s praise.
In these lone walls (their days eternal bound)
These moss-grown domes with spiry turrets crownï¿½d,
Where awful arches make a noonday night,
And the dim windows shed a solemn light;
Thy eyes diffusï¿½d a reconciling ray,
And gleams of glory brightenï¿½d all the day.
But now no face divine contentment wears,
ï¿½Tis all blank sadness, or continual tears.
See how the force of othersï¿½ prayï¿½rs I try,
(O pious fraud of amï¿½rous charity!)
But why should I on othersï¿½ prayï¿½rs depend?
Come thou, my father, brother, husband, friend!
Ah let thy handmaid, sister, daughter move,
And all those tender names in one, thy love!
The darksome pines that oï¿½er yon rocks reclinï¿½d
Wave high, and murmur to the hollow wind,
The wandï¿½ring streams that shine between the hills,
The grots that echo to the tinkling rills,
The dying gales that pant upon the trees,
The lakes that quiver to the curling breeze;
No more these scenes my meditation aid,
Or lull to rest the visionary maid.
But oï¿½er the twilight groves and dusky caves,
Long-sounding aisles, and intermingled graves,
Black Melancholy sits, and round her throws
A death-like silence, and a dread repose:
Her gloomy presence saddens all the scene,
Shades evï¿½ry flowï¿½r, and darkens evï¿½ry green,
Deepens the murmur of the falling floods,
And breathes a browner horror on the woods.

Yet here for ever, ever must I stay;
Sad proof how well a lover can obey!
Death, only death, can break the lasting chain;
And here, evï¿½n then, shall my cold dust remain,
Here all its frailties, all its flames resign,
And wait till ï¿½tis no sin to mix with thine.

Ah wretch! believï¿½d the spouse of God in vain,
Confessï¿½d within the slave of love and man.
Assist me, Heavï¿½n! but whence arose that prayï¿½r?
Sprung it from piety, or from despair?
Evï¿½n here, where frozen chastity retires,
Love finds an altar for forbidden fires.
I ought to grieve, but cannot what I ought;
I mourn the lover, not lament the fault;
I view my crime, but kindle at the view,
Repent old pleasures, and solicit new;
Now turnï¿½d to Heavï¿½n, I weep my past offence,
Now think of thee, and curse my innocence.
Of all affliction taught a lover yet,
ï¿½Tis sure the hardest science to forget!
How shall I lose the sin, yet keep the sense,
And love thï¿½ offender, yet detest thï¿½ offence?
How the dear object from the crime remove,
Or how distinguish penitence from love?
Unequal task! a passion to resign,
For hearts so touchï¿½d, so piercï¿½d, so lost as mine.
Ere such a soul regains its peaceful state,
How often must it love, how often hate!
How often hope, despair, resent, regret,
Conceal, disdainï¿½do all things but forget.
But let Heavï¿½n seize it, all at once ï¿½tis firï¿½d;
Not touchï¿½d, but rapt; not wakenï¿½d, but inspirï¿½d!
Oh come! oh teach me nature to subdue,
Renounce my love, my life, myselfï¿½and you.
Fill my fond heart with God alone, for he
Alone can rival, can succeed to thee.

How happy is the blameless vestalï¿½s lot!
The world forgetting, by the world forgot.
Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind!
Each prayï¿½r accepted, and each wish resignï¿½d;
Labour and rest, that equal periods keep;
ï¿½Obedient slumbers that can wake and weep;ï¿½
Desires composï¿½d, affections ever evï¿½n,
Tears that delight, and sighs that waft to Heavï¿½n.
Grace shines around her with serenest beams,
And whispï¿½ring angels prompt her golden dreams.
For her thï¿½ unfading rose of Eden blooms,
And wings of seraphs shed divine perfumes,
For her the Spouse prepares the bridal ring,
For her white virgins hymeneals sing,
To sounds of heavï¿½nly harps she dies away,
And melts in visions of eternal day.

Far other dreams my erring soul employ,
Far other raptures, of unholy joy:
When at the close of each sad, sorrowing day,
Fancy restores what vengeance snatchï¿½d away,
Then conscience sleeps, and leaving nature free,
All my loose soul unbounded springs to thee.
Oh cursï¿½d, dear horrors of all-conscious night!
How glowing guilt exalts the keen delight!
Provoking Daemons all restraint remove,
And stir within me every source of love.
I hear thee, view thee, gaze oï¿½er all thy charms,
And round thy phantom glue my clasping arms.
I wakeï¿½no more I hear, no more I view,
The phantom flies me, as unkind as you.
I call aloud; it hears not what I say;
I stretch my empty arms; it glides away.
To dream once more I close my willing eyes;
Ye soft illusions, dear deceits, arise!
Alas, no moreï¿½methinks we wandï¿½ring go
Through dreary wastes, and weep each otherï¿½s woe,
Where round some mouldï¿½ring tower pale ivy creeps,
And low-browï¿½d rocks hang nodding oï¿½er the deeps.
Sudden you mount, you beckon from the skies;
Clouds interpose, waves roar, and winds arise.
I shriek, start up, the same sad prospect find,
And wake to all the griefs I left behind.

For thee the fates, severely kind, ordain
A cool suspense from pleasure and from pain;
Thy life a long, dead calm of fixï¿½d repose;
No pulse that riots, and no blood that glows.
Still as the sea, ere winds were taught to blow,
Or moving spirit bade the waters flow;
Soft as the slumbers of a saint forgivï¿½n,
And mild as opening gleams of promisï¿½d heavï¿½n.

Come, Abelard! for what hast thou to dread?
The torch of Venus burns not for the dead.
Nature stands checkï¿½d; Religion disapproves;
Evï¿½n thou art coldï¿½yet Eloisa loves.
Ah hopeless, lasting flames! like those that burn
To light the dead, and warm thï¿½ unfruitful urn.

What scenes appear whereï¿½er I turn my view?
The dear ideas, where I fly, pursue,
Rise in the grove, before the altar rise,
Stain all my soul, and wanton in my eyes.
I waste the matin lamp in sighs for thee,
Thy image steals between my God and me,
Thy voice I seem in evï¿½ry hymn to hear,
With evï¿½ry bead I drop too soft a tear.
When from the censer clouds of fragrance roll,
And swelling organs lift the rising soul,
One thought of thee puts all the pomp to flight,
Priests, tapers, temples, swim before my sight:
In seas of flame my plunging soul is drownï¿½d,
While altars blaze, and angels tremble round.

While prostrate here in humble grief I lie,
Kind, virtuous drops just gathï¿½ring in my eye,
While praying, trembling, in the dust I roll,
And dawning grace is opï¿½ning on my soul:
Come, if thou darï¿½st, all charming as thou art!
Oppose thyself to Heavï¿½n; dispute my heart;
Come, with one glance of those deluding eyes
Blot out each bright idea of the skies;
Take back that grace, those sorrows, and those tears;
Take back my fruitless penitence and prayï¿½rs;
Snatch me, just mounting, from the blest abode;
Assist the fiends, and tear me from my God!

No, fly me, fly me, far as pole from pole;
Rise Alps between us! and whole oceans roll!
Ah, come not, write not, think not once of me,
Nor share one pang of all I felt for thee.
Thy oaths I quit, thy memory resign;
Forget, renounce me, hate whateï¿½er was mine.
Fair eyes, and tempting looks (which yet I view!)
Long lovï¿½d, adorï¿½d ideas, all adieu!
Oh Grace serene! oh virtue heavï¿½nly fair!
Divine oblivion of low-thoughted care!
Fresh blooming hope, gay daughter of the sky!
And faith, our early immortality!
Enter, each mild, each amicable guest;
Receive, and wrap me in eternal rest!

See in her cell sad Eloisa spread,
Proppï¿½d on some tomb, a neighbour of the dead.
In each low wind methinks a spirit calls,
And more than echoes talk along the walls.
Here, as I watchï¿½d the dying lamps around,
From yonder shrine I heard a hollow sound.
ï¿½Come, sister, come!ï¿½ (it said, or seemï¿½d to say)
ï¿½Thy place is here, sad sister, come away!
Once like thyself, I trembled, wept, and prayï¿½d,
Loveï¿½s victim then, though now a sainted maid:
But all is calm in this eternal sleep;
Here grief forgets to groan, and love to weep,
Evï¿½n superstition loses evï¿½ry fear:
For God, not man, absolves our frailties here.ï¿½

I come, I come! prepare your roseate bowï¿½rs,
Celestial palms, and ever-blooming flowï¿½rs.
Thither, where sinners may have rest, I go,
Where flames refinï¿½d in breasts seraphic glow:
Thou, Abelard! the last sad office pay,
And smooth my passage to the realms of day;
See my lips tremble, and my eye-balls roll,
Suck my last breath, and catch my flying soul!
Ah noï¿½in sacred vestments mayï¿½st thou stand,
The hallowï¿½d taper trembling in thy hand,
Present the cross before my lifted eye,
Teach me at once, and learn of me to die.
Ah then, thy once-lovï¿½d Eloisa see!
It will be then no crime to gaze on me.
See from my cheek the transient roses fly!
See the last sparkle languish in my eye!
Till evï¿½ry motion, pulse, and breath be oï¿½er;
And evï¿½n my Abelard be lovï¿½d no more.
O Death all-eloquent! you only prove
What dust we dote on, when ï¿½tis man we love.

Then too, when fate shall thy fair frame destroy,
(That cause of all my guilt, and all my joy)
In trance ecstatic may thy pangs be drownï¿½d,
Bright clouds descend, and angels watch thee round,
From opï¿½ning skies may streaming glories shine,
And saints embrace thee with a love like mine.

May one kind grave unite each hapless name,
And graft my love immortal on thy fame!
Then, ages hence, when all my woes are oï¿½er,
When this rebellious heart shall beat no more;
If ever chance two wandï¿½ring lovers brings
To Paracleteï¿½s white walls and silver springs,
Oï¿½er the pale marble shall they join their heads,
And drink the falling tears each other sheds;
Then sadly say, with mutual pity movï¿½d,
ï¿½Oh may we never love as these have lovï¿½d!ï¿½

From the full choir when loud Hosannas rise,
And swell the pomp of dreadful sacrifice,
Amid that scene if some relenting eye
Glance on the stone where our cold relics lie,
Devotionï¿½s self shall steal a thought from Heavï¿½n,
One human tear shall drop and be forgivï¿½n.
And sure, if fate some future bard shall join
In sad similitude of griefs to mine,
Condemnï¿½d whole years in absence to deplore,
And image charms he must behold no more;
Such if there be, who loves so long, so well;
Let him our sad, our tender story tell;
The well-sung woes will soothe my pensive ghost;
He best can paint ï¿½em, who shall feel ï¿½em most.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Alexander Pope represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Alexander Pope's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
